message.content:
Adversarially verify this finding about a design doc (the doc is at /tmp/claude-1000/-home-jleechan-projects-worktree-runner/8ca589dd-8422-44b1-a543-52bd7b71f14b/scratchpad/gistfile1.txt; the repo is /home/jleechan/projects/worktree_runner; you may use WebSearch/WebFetch and read files). Try to REFUTE it. Finding from the "rust" reviewer, severity critical:
TITLE: Config generated via format!/string templates while serde+serde_yaml sit unused — typed config must be v1, not 'future work'
DETAIL: src/config.rs (lines 212–251) builds outrunner.yml by string interpolation (`format!(r#"..."#, ...)`) and a raw string literal, then the design's own 'Next Steps' item 3 (line 290) defers 'Better config model (proper structs + serialization)' to later. Meanwhile Cargo.toml pulls serde with derive and serde_yaml, and neither is imported anywhere in the skeleton. String-templated YAML is whitespace-fragile (the leading newline in the r#" block already produces a file starting with a blank line), cannot be validated, cannot round-trip for `status`/`validate` subcommands, and any user-influenced value (labels, counts, future runner names) becomes a YAML-injection vector. This is the single cheapest thing to get right on day one and the most expensive to retrofit.
RECOMMENDATION: Define `#[derive(Serialize, Deserialize)] struct OutrunnerConfig { runners: BTreeMap<String, RunnerSpec> }` with `RunnerSpec { backend: BackendKind, labels: Vec<String>, count: u32, resources: Resources }` and emit via the chosen serde YAML backend. Delete the format! templates. This also gives you `init --dry-run` (print serialized config) and config re-reading for free.
Is the finding factually correct and does the recommendation make sense? If the factual basis is wrong or the severity is inflated, refute it. Default to refuted=true only if you have concrete contrary evidence or the claim is unsupported after checking.

input.reasoning: All factual claims verified against the doc: (1) config.rs generate() spans exactly lines 212-251 and builds outrunner.yml via format!(r#"..."#) plus a raw string literal, written with std::fs::write; (2) Next Steps item 3 at line 290 defers 'Better config model (proper structs + serialization)'; (3) Cargo.toml (lines 90-91) declares serde with derive and serde_yaml 0.9, and no use statement in any skeleton module imports either; (4) both raw strings open with a newline after r#", so the emitted file genuinely starts with a blank line; (5) no deserialization path exists, so status/validate cannot round-trip config. The recommendation (serde-derived OutrunnerConfig/RunnerSpec structs, serialize via a serde YAML backend, delete format! templates) is standard practice and uses dependencies the doc already declares — the doc's own dependency list contradicts its deferral. Two minor overstatements do not rise to refutation: the leading blank line is cosmetic (valid YAML), and the YAML-injection claim is prospective (current interpolated values — backend literal and plat.os from cfg! — are program-controlled, not user-influenced), but the finding itself frames injection as a future-input risk ('labels, counts, future runner names'), which is fair for a design review. Severity 'critical' is at the aggressive end for a skeleton, but the retrofit-cost argument is coherent and the factual basis is fully correct, so the finding stands.
